title: Linear Algebra Notes
text:
A field is a set with two operations (addition and multiplication) that satisfy certain properties. The most important fields for linear algebra are: - \(\mathbb{R}\) (real numbers) - \(\mathbb{C}\) (complex numbers)



A vector space is a set \(V\) along with operations of addition and scalar multiplication that satisfy eight axioms:
Addition axioms: 1. Commutativity: \(u + v = v + u\) 2. Associativity: \((u + v) + w = u + (v + w)\) 3. Additive identity: \(\exists 0 \in V\) such that \(v + 0 = v\) 4. Additive inverse: \(\forall v \in V, \exists -v\) such that \(v + (-v) = 0\)
Scalar multiplication axioms: 5. Distributivity: \(a(u + v) = au + av\) 6. Distributivity: \((a + b)v = av + bv\) 7. Associativity: \(a(bv) = (ab)v\) 8. Multiplicative identity: \(1v = v\)




\(\mathbb{F}^n\): The set of all \(n\)-tuples with entries from field \(\mathbb{F}\)
Function spaces: \(\mathbb{F}^S\) - functions from set \(S\) to field \(\mathbb{F}\)
Polynomial spaces: \(\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{F})\) - all polynomials with coefficients in \(\mathbb{F}\)







Linear combination: A vector \(v\) is a linear combination of vectors \(v_1, \ldots, v_m\) if: \[v = a_1v_1 + \cdots + a_mv_m\] for some scalars \(a_1, \ldots, a_m\).
Span: The span of vectors \(v_1, \ldots, v_m\) is the set of all linear combinations: \[\text{span}(v_1, \ldots, v_m) = \{a_1v_1 + \cdots + a_mv_m : a_1, \ldots, a_m \in \mathbb{F}\}\]
Linear independence: Vectors \(v_1, \ldots, v_m\) are linearly independent if: \[a_1v_1 + \cdots + a_mv_m = 0 \implies a_1 = \cdots = a_m = 0\]



A basis of vector space \(V\) is a list of vectors that: 1. Spans \(V\) 2. Is linearly independent
Key theorem: Every finite-dimensional vector space has a basis.



The dimension of a finite-dimensional vector space is the length of any basis.
Important properties: - All bases of a vector space have the same length - \(\dim(\mathbb{F}^n) = n\) - \(\dim(\mathcal{P}_m(\mathbb{F})) = m + 1\)






A linear map from \(V\) to \(W\) is a function \(T: V \to W\) such that: 1. Additivity: \(T(u + v) = Tu + Tv\) 2. Homogeneity: \(T(av) = aTv\)



For linear map \(T: V \to W\):
Null space: \(\text{null } T = \{v \in V : Tv = 0\}\)
Range: \(\text{range } T = \{Tv : v \in V\}\)
Fundamental theorem: If \(V\) is finite-dimensional, then: \[\dim V = \dim(\text{null } T) + \dim(\text{range } T)\]

Note: This is a sample of the notes structure. Each chapter would be expanded with detailed explanations, examples, and proofs.










NoteCommunity Discussion

title: Linear Algebra Notes
text:
A field is a set with two operations (addition and multiplication) that satisfy certain properties. The most important fields for linear algebra are: - \(\mathbb{R}\) (real numbers) - \(\mathbb{C}\) (complex numbers)



A vector space is a set \(V\) along with operations of addition and scalar multiplication that satisfy eight axioms:
Addition axioms: 1. Commutativity: \(u + v = v + u\) 2. Associativity: \((u + v) + w = u + (v + w)\) 3. Additive identity: \(\exists 0 \in V\) such that \(v + 0 = v\) 4. Additive inverse: \(\forall v \in V, \exists -v\) such that \(v + (-v) = 0\)
Scalar multiplication axioms: 5. Distributivity: \(a(u + v) = au + av\) 6. Distributivity: \((a + b)v = av + bv\) 7. Associativity: \(a(bv) = (ab)v\) 8. Multiplicative identity: \(1v = v\)




\(\mathbb{F}^n\): The set of all \(n\)-tuples with entries from field \(\mathbb{F}\)
Function spaces: \(\mathbb{F}^S\) - functions from set \(S\) to field \(\mathbb{F}\)
Polynomial spaces: \(\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{F})\) - all polynomials with coefficients in \(\mathbb{F}\)

title: Linear Algebra Notes
text:
Linear combination: A vector \(v\) is a linear combination of vectors \(v_1, \ldots, v_m\) if: \[v = a_1v_1 + \cdots + a_mv_m\] for some scalars \(a_1, \ldots, a_m\).
Span: The span of vectors \(v_1, \ldots, v_m\) is the set of all linear combinations: \[\text{span}(v_1, \ldots, v_m) = \{a_1v_1 + \cdots + a_mv_m : a_1, \ldots, a_m \in \mathbb{F}\}\]
Linear independence: Vectors \(v_1, \ldots, v_m\) are linearly independent if: \[a_1v_1 + \cdots + a_mv_m = 0 \implies a_1 = \cdots = a_m = 0\]



A basis of vector space \(V\) is a list of vectors that: 1. Spans \(V\) 2. Is linearly independent
Key theorem: Every finite-dimensional vector space has a basis.



The dimension of a finite-dimensional vector space is the length of any basis.
Important properties: - All bases of a vector space have the same length - \(\dim(\mathbb{F}^n) = n\) - \(\dim(\mathcal{P}_m(\mathbb{F})) = m + 1\)

title: Linear Algebra Notes
text:
A linear map from \(V\) to \(W\) is a function \(T: V \to W\) such that: 1. Additivity: \(T(u + v) = Tu + Tv\) 2. Homogeneity: \(T(av) = aTv\)



For linear map \(T: V \to W\):
Null space: \(\text{null } T = \{v \in V : Tv = 0\}\)
Range: \(\text{range } T = \{Tv : v \in V\}\)
Fundamental theorem: If \(V\) is finite-dimensional, then: \[\dim V = \dim(\text{null } T) + \dim(\text{range } T)\]

Note: This is a sample of the notes structure. Each chapter would be expanded with detailed explanations, examples, and proofs.

title: Linear Algebra Exercises
text:
Prove that \(-(-v) = v\) for every \(v\) in a vector space \(V\).






TipSolution





Let \(v \in V\). We know that \(-v\) is the additive inverse of \(v\), so \(v + (-v) = 0\).
Since \(-v \in V\), it has an additive inverse, which we denote as \(-(-v)\). By definition: \((-v) + (-(-v)) = 0\).
Now, consider: \[v + (-v) = 0 = (-v) + (-(-v))\]
Adding \(-(-v)\) to both sides: \[-(-v) + v + (-v) = -(-v) + (-v) + (-(-v))\] \[-(-v) + 0 = -(-v) + 0\] \[-(-v) = -(-v)\]
This is always true. Let’s try a different approach.
From \(v + (-v) = 0\), we can add \(-(-v)\) to both sides: \[-(-v) + v + (-v) = -(-v) + 0\] \[(-(-v) + v) + (-v) = -(-v)\]
Since \((-v) + (-(-v)) = 0\), we have \(-(-v) = -(-v)\).
Actually, let’s use uniqueness of additive inverse: Since \(v + (-v) = 0\) and \((-v) + (-(-v)) = 0\), and the additive inverse is unique, we must have \(v = -(-v)\).






Suppose \(v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4\) spans \(V\). Prove that the list \(v_1 - v_2, v_2 - v_3, v_3 - v_4, v_4\) also spans \(V\).






TipSolution





We need to show that every vector in \(V\) can be written as a linear combination of \(v_1 - v_2, v_2 - v_3, v_3 - v_4, v_4\).
Since \(v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4\) spans \(V\), any \(v \in V\) can be written as: \[v = a_1v_1 + a_2v_2 + a_3v_3 + a_4v_4\]
We need to express \(v_1, v_2, v_3\) in terms of our new list: - \(v_4 = v_4\) - \(v_3 = (v_3 - v_4) + v_4\) - \(v_2 = (v_2 - v_3) + v_3 = (v_2 - v_3) + (v_3 - v_4) + v_4\) - \(v_1 = (v_1 - v_2) + v_2 = (v_1 - v_2) + (v_2 - v_3) + (v_3 - v_4) + v_4\)
Therefore: \[v = a_1[(v_1 - v_2) + (v_2 - v_3) + (v_3 - v_4) + v_4] + a_2[(v_2 - v_3) + (v_3 - v_4) + v_4] + a_3[(v_3 - v_4) + v_4] + a_4v_4\]
Collecting terms: \[v = a_1(v_1 - v_2) + (a_1 + a_2)(v_2 - v_3) + (a_1 + a_2 + a_3)(v_3 - v_4) + (a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + a_4)v_4\]
This shows that \(v\) is in the span of \(v_1 - v_2, v_2 - v_3, v_3 - v_4, v_4\).









Find a basis for the vector space of polynomials in \(\mathcal{P}_3(\mathbb{R})\) that have the property that \(p(2) = 0\).






TipSolution





We need polynomials \(p(x) = a_0 + a_1x + a_2x^2 + a_3x^3\) such that \(p(2) = 0\).
This gives us: \(a_0 + 2a_1 + 4a_2 + 8a_3 = 0\), so \(a_0 = -2a_1 - 4a_2 - 8a_3\).
Therefore: \(p(x) = (-2a_1 - 4a_2 - 8a_3) + a_1x + a_2x^2 + a_3x^3\) \(= a_1(x - 2) + a_2(x^2 - 4) + a_3(x^3 - 8)\)
A basis is: \(\{x - 2, x^2 - 4, x^3 - 8\}\)
We can verify: - These polynomials are linearly independent (check by setting linear combination to zero) - They span the space (any polynomial with \(p(2) = 0\) can be written in this form) - The dimension is 3 (which makes sense: we have one constraint on a 4-dimensional space)






Prove that if \(U_1, U_2, W\) are subspaces of \(V\) such that \(V = U_1 \oplus W = U_2 \oplus W\), then \(U_1 = U_2\).






TipSolution





We need to show \(U_1 \subseteq U_2\) and \(U_2 \subseteq U_1\).
Let \(u_1 \in U_1\). Since \(V = U_2 \oplus W\), we can write: \[u_1 = u_2 + w\] for unique \(u_2 \in U_2\) and \(w \in W\).
Since \(V = U_1 \oplus W\), we also have: \[u_1 = u_1 + 0\] where \(u_1 \in U_1\) and \(0 \in W\).
By uniqueness of the direct sum decomposition \(V = U_1 \oplus W\): - The \(U_1\) component is \(u_1\) - The \(W\) component is \(0\)
But we also wrote \(u_1 = u_2 + w\) where \(u_2 \in U_2\) and \(w \in W\). Since \(u_1 \in U_1\) and \(V = U_1 \oplus W\), we must have \(w = 0\) and the \(U_1\) component equals \(u_2\).
Therefore \(u_1 = u_2 \in U_2\), so \(U_1 \subseteq U_2\).
By symmetry, \(U_2 \subseteq U_1\), so \(U_1 = U_2\).









Give an example of a linear map \(T: \mathbb{R}^4 \to \mathbb{R}^2\) such that \(\dim(\text{null } T) = 2\).






TipSolution





By the fundamental theorem of linear maps: \[\dim \mathbb{R}^4 = \dim(\text{null } T) + \dim(\text{range } T)\] \[4 = 2 + \dim(\text{range } T)\]
So \(\dim(\text{range } T) = 2\).
Define \(T: \mathbb{R}^4 \to \mathbb{R}^2\) by: \[T(x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4) = (x_1 + x_2, x_3 + x_4)\]
Then: - \(\text{null } T = \{(x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4) : x_1 + x_2 = 0 \text{ and } x_3 + x_4 = 0\}\) - \(= \{(a, -a, b, -b) : a, b \in \mathbb{R}\}\) - \(= \text{span}((1, -1, 0, 0), (0, 0, 1, -1))\)
So \(\dim(\text{null } T) = 2\) as required.




Note: This represents a sample of exercises. A complete version would include many more problems from each chapter, with varying difficulty levels and comprehensive solutions.

title: Linear Algebra Exercises
text:
Prove that \(-(-v) = v\) for every \(v\) in a vector space \(V\).






TipSolution





Let \(v \in V\). We know that \(-v\) is the additive inverse of \(v\), so \(v + (-v) = 0\).
Since \(-v \in V\), it has an additive inverse, which we denote as \(-(-v)\). By definition: \((-v) + (-(-v)) = 0\).
Now, consider: \[v + (-v) = 0 = (-v) + (-(-v))\]
Adding \(-(-v)\) to both sides: \[-(-v) + v + (-v) = -(-v) + (-v) + (-(-v))\] \[-(-v) + 0 = -(-v) + 0\] \[-(-v) = -(-v)\]
This is always true. Let’s try a different approach.
From \(v + (-v) = 0\), we can add \(-(-v)\) to both sides: \[-(-v) + v + (-v) = -(-v) + 0\] \[(-(-v) + v) + (-v) = -(-v)\]
Since \((-v) + (-(-v)) = 0\), we have \(-(-v) = -(-v)\).
Actually, let’s use uniqueness of additive inverse: Since \(v + (-v) = 0\) and \((-v) + (-(-v)) = 0\), and the additive inverse is unique, we must have \(v = -(-v)\).






Suppose \(v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4\) spans \(V\). Prove that the list \(v_1 - v_2, v_2 - v_3, v_3 - v_4, v_4\) also spans \(V\).






TipSolution





We need to show that every vector in \(V\) can be written as a linear combination of \(v_1 - v_2, v_2 - v_3, v_3 - v_4, v_4\).
Since \(v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4\) spans \(V\), any \(v \in V\) can be written as: \[v = a_1v_1 + a_2v_2 + a_3v_3 + a_4v_4\]
We need to express \(v_1, v_2, v_3\) in terms of our new list: - \(v_4 = v_4\) - \(v_3 = (v_3 - v_4) + v_4\) - \(v_2 = (v_2 - v_3) + v_3 = (v_2 - v_3) + (v_3 - v_4) + v_4\) - \(v_1 = (v_1 - v_2) + v_2 = (v_1 - v_2) + (v_2 - v_3) + (v_3 - v_4) + v_4\)
Therefore: \[v = a_1[(v_1 - v_2) + (v_2 - v_3) + (v_3 - v_4) + v_4] + a_2[(v_2 - v_3) + (v_3 - v_4) + v_4] + a_3[(v_3 - v_4) + v_4] + a_4v_4\]
Collecting terms: \[v = a_1(v_1 - v_2) + (a_1 + a_2)(v_2 - v_3) + (a_1 + a_2 + a_3)(v_3 - v_4) + (a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + a_4)v_4\]
This shows that \(v\) is in the span of \(v_1 - v_2, v_2 - v_3, v_3 - v_4, v_4\).

title: Linear Algebra Exercises
text:
Find a basis for the vector space of polynomials in \(\mathcal{P}_3(\mathbb{R})\) that have the property that \(p(2) = 0\).






TipSolution





We need polynomials \(p(x) = a_0 + a_1x + a_2x^2 + a_3x^3\) such that \(p(2) = 0\).
This gives us: \(a_0 + 2a_1 + 4a_2 + 8a_3 = 0\), so \(a_0 = -2a_1 - 4a_2 - 8a_3\).
Therefore: \(p(x) = (-2a_1 - 4a_2 - 8a_3) + a_1x + a_2x^2 + a_3x^3\) \(= a_1(x - 2) + a_2(x^2 - 4) + a_3(x^3 - 8)\)
A basis is: \(\{x - 2, x^2 - 4, x^3 - 8\}\)
We can verify: - These polynomials are linearly independent (check by setting linear combination to zero) - They span the space (any polynomial with \(p(2) = 0\) can be written in this form) - The dimension is 3 (which makes sense: we have one constraint on a 4-dimensional space)






Prove that if \(U_1, U_2, W\) are subspaces of \(V\) such that \(V = U_1 \oplus W = U_2 \oplus W\), then \(U_1 = U_2\).






TipSolution





We need to show \(U_1 \subseteq U_2\) and \(U_2 \subseteq U_1\).
Let \(u_1 \in U_1\). Since \(V = U_2 \oplus W\), we can write: \[u_1 = u_2 + w\] for unique \(u_2 \in U_2\) and \(w \in W\).
Since \(V = U_1 \oplus W\), we also have: \[u_1 = u_1 + 0\] where \(u_1 \in U_1\) and \(0 \in W\).
By uniqueness of the direct sum decomposition \(V = U_1 \oplus W\): - The \(U_1\) component is \(u_1\) - The \(W\) component is \(0\)
But we also wrote \(u_1 = u_2 + w\) where \(u_2 \in U_2\) and \(w \in W\). Since \(u_1 \in U_1\) and \(V = U_1 \oplus W\), we must have \(w = 0\) and the \(U_1\) component equals \(u_2\).
Therefore \(u_1 = u_2 \in U_2\), so \(U_1 \subseteq U_2\).
By symmetry, \(U_2 \subseteq U_1\), so \(U_1 = U_2\).

title: Linear Algebra Exercises
text:
Give an example of a linear map \(T: \mathbb{R}^4 \to \mathbb{R}^2\) such that \(\dim(\text{null } T) = 2\).






TipSolution





By the fundamental theorem of linear maps: \[\dim \mathbb{R}^4 = \dim(\text{null } T) + \dim(\text{range } T)\] \[4 = 2 + \dim(\text{range } T)\]
So \(\dim(\text{range } T) = 2\).
Define \(T: \mathbb{R}^4 \to \mathbb{R}^2\) by: \[T(x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4) = (x_1 + x_2, x_3 + x_4)\]
Then: - \(\text{null } T = \{(x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4) : x_1 + x_2 = 0 \text{ and } x_3 + x_4 = 0\}\) - \(= \{(a, -a, b, -b) : a, b \in \mathbb{R}\}\) - \(= \text{span}((1, -1, 0, 0), (0, 0, 1, -1))\)
So \(\dim(\text{null } T) = 2\) as required.




Note: This represents a sample of exercises. A complete version would include many more problems from each chapter, with varying difficulty levels and comprehensive solutions.

title: Welcome to Knowledge Hub
text:
This is a comprehensive knowledge repository designed to be your go-to resource for various academic subjects. Our goal is to provide well-organized, accessible content that serves both as a learning tool and reference material.




Our mathematics section covers fundamental and advanced topics through carefully curated content based on renowned textbooks. Each section includes:

Comprehensive Notes: Detailed explanations and key concepts
Practice Exercises: Problems to reinforce understanding
Interactive Comments: Community discussions and clarifications





Linear Algebra: Based on “Linear Algebra Done Right” by Sheldon Axler





Use the navigation bar above to explore different subjects, or browse through the sidebar for specific topics within each subject area.



This knowledge hub is designed to grow over time. Each page includes a comment section where you can: - Ask questions about the material - Share insights and alternative explanations - Discuss problem-solving approaches - Suggest improvements or corrections

Happy learning!

title: Mathematics
text:
Welcome to the Mathematics section of our knowledge hub. Here you’ll find comprehensive coverage of various mathematical topics, organized by textbook and subject area.




Based on: “Linear Algebra Done Right” by Sheldon Axler
This section provides a rigorous introduction to linear algebra, focusing on: - Vector spaces and subspaces - Linear independence and bases - Linear maps and matrices - Eigenvalues and eigenvectors - Inner product spaces - Operators on inner product spaces
Explore Linear Algebra →




We’re continuously expanding our mathematics coverage. Future sections will include: - Real Analysis - Abstract Algebra - Differential Equations - Probability Theory - Number Theory



Each mathematical topic is organized into:

Overview Pages: Introduction and roadmap for the topic
Notes: Detailed explanations of concepts, theorems, and proofs
Exercises: Practice problems with varying difficulty levels
Comments: Community discussion for each page





Start with the overview to understand the big picture
Work through notes systematically
Attempt exercises to test your understanding
Use the comment sections to ask questions or share insights


Mathematics is not about numbers, equations, computations, or algorithms: it is about understanding. - William Paul Thurston

title: Linear Algebra
text:
This section follows Sheldon Axler’s “Linear Algebra Done Right,” which takes a unique approach to linear algebra by: - Emphasizing vector spaces and linear maps over matrices - Avoiding determinants until the end - Focusing on understanding rather than computation






Vector Spaces: Definition, examples, and basic properties
Finite-Dimensional Vector Spaces: Span, linear independence, and bases
Linear Maps: Definition, null spaces, and ranges





Polynomials: Background for understanding characteristic polynomials
Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors: Finding and understanding eigenvalues
Upper-Triangular Matrices: Simplifying linear operators





Inner Products: Norms, orthogonality, and orthonormal bases
Operators on Inner Product Spaces: Self-adjoint and normal operators
Operators on Complex Vector Spaces: Spectral theorem
Operators on Real Vector Spaces: Special cases and applications






Notes: Comprehensive chapter-by-chapter notes
Exercises: Practice problems and solutions





Start Here: Read through the notes for each chapter
Practice: Work through exercises to reinforce concepts
Discuss: Use comment sections to ask questions or share insights
Review: Revisit challenging topics as needed





Understanding vector spaces abstractly
Linear independence and spanning sets
Null space and range of linear maps
Eigenvalues and their geometric meaning
Inner products and orthogonality
Spectral theorem and its applications


The purpose of computation is insight, not numbers. - Richard Hamming

title: Comments Demo
text:
This page demonstrates what the comment section will look like when properly configured with Giscus.


Here’s some sample mathematical content:
Theorem: For any vector space \(V\) over field \(\mathbb{F}\), the zero vector is unique.
Proof: Suppose there are two zero vectors \(0_1\) and \(0_2\). Then: \[0_1 = 0_1 + 0_2 = 0_2\]
The first equality uses the fact that \(0_2\) is a zero vector, and the second uses the fact that \(0_1\) is a zero vector.
